rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the source text and includes a quantified mitigation outcome. policy_statement: "behavioural measures to reduce electricity use (targeting a 10% cut)" indicates direct energy-demand reduction (mitigation). verbatim_text repeatedly targets "save energy" / "saving energy" through staff behavior change and an internal challenge. Given climate_screen = explicit_self, the row should be kept despite the token-gate failure ("no Mitigation outcome token found").
policy_statement: For municipal buildings, the Municipality will implement a staff awareness campaign and behavioural measures to reduce electricity use (targeting a 10% cut) and promote an internal challenge supported by the Environmental Awareness Unit (EAU).
mechanism_description: The municipality plans to raise staff awareness and run an internal challenge to encourage behaviour changes in municipal buildings that reduce electricity use and related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Human behavior in buildings → ↓ electricity use → ↓ fossil generation/combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Municipal staff/occupants in municipal buildings adopt energy-saving behaviors (e.g., switching off lights and IT equipment, reducing standby loads, managing HVAC setpoints and schedules, conserving during peak periods), often reinforced through campaigns and competitions.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced end-use electricity consumption lowers set electricity generation on the grid (and may lower peak load), decreasing fuel combustion and associated CO₂ (and other) emissions at power plants.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions attributable to municipal building electricity use (and sometimes ancillary reductions in waste heat and local air pollutants).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced electricity demand leading to lower grid GHG emissions (and potentially reduced peak demand).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Behavior change reduces electricity consumption in buildings, which reduces grid energy production and thus GHG emissions—this is a direct emissions-reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although it can improve “resource efficiency” (using less energy for the same service), the dominant climate-relevant intent and effect is emissions mitigation via reduced energy demand; adaptation benefits (e.g., comfort during heat) are not the primary causal chain described.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a campaign primarily targets resilience behaviors (e.g., heatwave safety) it would shift toward Adaptation, but this mechanism explicitly targets electricity reduction and emissions. If paired with major retrofits, the retrofit mechanism would be separate; here the lever is behavioral/operational change.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy using this canonical mechanism (behavior-change campaigns aimed at reducing building energy use) primarily reduces energy demand and associated GHG emissions, fitting Mitigation across contexts regardless of grid mix (magnitude varies but direction is consistent).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further subtype (e.g., demand-side management, public sector leadership, peak reduction) depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the electricity grid is already near-zero carbon, the GHG mitigation impact may be smaller and the “resource efficiency” framing could become more salient; however, the dominant intended climate pathway of this mechanism remains emissions mitigation via reduced energy demand.
key_indicators: "raising staff awareness"; "simple change of behaviour to save energy"; "promote a challenge between services/units"; "best performing service would be celebrated"; "energy saver/climate saver"; "special gratification"; "saving energy"
co_benefits: Reduced energy bills/operational costs; potential local air quality benefits from reduced power generation; improved staff engagement
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a minor incentive element ("special gratification"), but the core instrument is an awareness/behavior campaign and internal challenge; classified under information-disclosure rather than incentive-program because no structured financial subsidy/rebate scheme is specified.
